lead: Packing is the part of a move that quietly decides how the whole day goes. Get it right and our crews can load a home in a morning; get it wrong and even a short hop across Ipswich turns into a long, fraught afternoon. Since 2005 our team here in Westerfield has packed thousands of Suffolk homes, and we've learned the small, practical habits that make the difference. These are the tips we actually use ourselves, shared plainly so you can pack like a professional and arrive with everything safe.

sections.h: Start Early and Work One Room at a Time
sections.body:
The most common mistake we see is leaving everything until the last few days, then trying to pack the whole house in a panic. Packing always takes longer than people expect, and rushing is how things get broken or lost. In our experience the calmest moves are the ones where boxing began two or three weeks out, starting with the rooms and items you barely touch.

Work through the house one room at a time rather than darting about. Finish the spare bedroom completely, seal and label those boxes, then move on. This keeps your home liveable as you go and stops half-packed clutter spreading into every corner. It also makes the job feel achievable; a single room in an evening is far less daunting than a whole house in a weekend.

Begin with the loft, the shed and the garage, because those spaces always hold more than anyone remembers and are the ones people forget until the lorry is half loaded. Tackle the kitchen and the everyday essentials last, since you'll need them right up to moving morning. By the time our crew arrives, you want everything boxed except the kettle and the breakfast things, ready to load straight onto the van.

sections.h: Use the Right Box for the Job
sections.body:
Not every box is meant to carry everything, and the way you fill them matters more than people think. The golden rule our crews live by is heavy items in small boxes, light items in large ones. A small box crammed with books is liftable; a large box packed the same way becomes a back-breaker that often splits its base on the way to the van.

Fill every box properly. A box that is part full will crush when others are stacked on it in the lorry, while one packed too proud won't sit flat or close cleanly. Aim to fill each one to the brim, then top off any gaps with scrunched paper or soft items like tea towels and jumpers so nothing shifts in transit. A well-packed box should feel solid when you give it a gentle shake.

Always tape the base properly before you start filling. We use a strip along the centre seam and one across each end, which stops the bottom giving way at the worst possible moment. Specialist boxes earn their keep too: wardrobe boxes let clothes travel on their hangers, and double-walled boxes are worth it for anything heavy or precious. Spending a little on decent boxes saves a great deal of grief.

sections.h: Protect the Things You Care About Most
sections.body:
Every home has items that matter beyond their price, and these deserve a little extra thought. Wrap anything breakable individually rather than bundling several pieces together, and never let two hard objects touch inside a box without padding between them. Glasses, ceramics and ornaments each want their own wrap so they cannot knock against one another as the van moves.

Plates travel best standing on their edges rather than stacked flat, which sounds counterintuitive but genuinely resists pressure far better. Cushion the bottom of the box with crumpled paper, stand the plates upright like records in a rack, and pack soft items around the sides. For pictures and mirrors, tape a cross of masking tape across the glass and wrap the frame well, then store them upright between mattresses or against a wall, never lying flat where they can crack.

Take a moment to set aside genuinely irreplaceable things, such as passports, jewellery, photographs and important paperwork. These should travel in your own car rather than on the lorry, in a clearly marked bag you keep with you all day. Although our crews handle everything with care and your belongings are fully covered while in our hands, some items simply give you peace of mind kept close.

sections.h: Label Clearly and Keep an Inventory
sections.body:
A box without a label is a guessing game waiting to happen. Write the destination room on the side of every box, not just the top, because once boxes are stacked the tops disappear and only the sides remain visible. Add a quick note of the contents while you're at it, so you know at a glance what's inside without tearing it open.

Our crews unload straight to the right rooms when boxes are labelled well, which saves you a mountain of fetching and carrying later. A colour-coding system works brilliantly: assign each room a colour, put a matching note on the door at the new house, and stick the right colour on each box. Even a team who has never seen your home can then unload at speed.

Keep a simple numbered list of what's in each box too. It feels fussy while you're doing it, but if anything ends up in storage, or your move runs over two days, that inventory lets you find the slow cooker or the spare duvet without opening half the boxes in the house. Mark fragile boxes boldly on more than one side, and note which ones you'll want first so we can load them last and unload them first.

sections.h: Pack an Essentials Box You Keep With You
sections.body:
If you take only one tip from us, take this one, because it's the thing our customers thank us for most. Before anything else gets boxed, pack a single, clearly marked essentials box and keep it in your own car rather than on the lorry. This is the box that keeps everyone going through a long day and into the first night.

Into it goes the kettle, a few mugs, tea, milk and biscuits, phone chargers, basic toiletries, a change of clothes, any medication, the important documents and a few toys if you have little ones. When you arrive tired and surrounded by sealed boxes, being able to make a brew and find a toothbrush without unpacking the whole van is worth its weight in gold.

Treat that box as untouchable and don't let it get loaded by mistake; mark it boldly so nobody is tempted to be helpful and carry it to the van. If your move spans a couple of days, or there's any chance of a delay in the chain, pack it a little fuller with bin bags, kitchen roll, scissors, a torch and a small first aid kit. Keep it on the passenger seat where you can see it.

sections.h: Know When to Let the Professionals Pack
sections.body:
There's no shame in handing the packing over, and for many of our Suffolk customers it's the best money they spend on the whole move. A professional packing service comes into its own when time is short, when the move is large, or when a home holds a lot of fragile, antique or awkward items that need proper care and the right materials.

Our crews pack a room in a fraction of the time it takes most households, partly through practice and partly because we arrive with everything needed: the right boxes, proper wrapping, tape and protective covers all to hand. We pack methodically, label as we go and keep an inventory, so nothing is missed and everything lands in the right room at the other end. Your belongings are fully covered while we handle them, which puts many minds at rest.

You don't have to choose all or nothing, either. Plenty of our customers pack the straightforward rooms themselves and ask us to handle the kitchen, the loft or a treasured display cabinet, where the breakables and the fiddly items live. Whatever the split, do tell us in advance so we bring the right materials and the right number of crew, and your packing and your moving day both run to time.

faqs: How far in advance should I start packing?

Start two to three weeks before the move, beginning with rooms and items you rarely use, such as the loft, shed and spare room. Leave the kitchen and everyday essentials until last. Early, steady packing is far calmer than a last-minute rush and means far less gets broken or mislaid.
What should I put heavy items in?

Always pack heavy things like books and crockery in small boxes, and light, bulky items such as bedding and cushions in large ones. A large box full of heavy items is hard to lift safely and likely to split its base. Fill every box fully so it doesn't crush when stacked in the van.
How do I stop fragile items breaking in transit?

Wrap each breakable piece individually, never letting two hard items touch without padding between them. Stand plates on their edges rather than stacking them flat, cushion the box base and sides, and mark the box fragile on more than one side. Keep truly irreplaceable items in your own car.
Should I pay for a professional packing service?

It's well worth it if time is short, the move is large, or you have many fragile or antique items. Our crews pack quickly with the right materials, label and inventory as they go, and your belongings are fully covered throughout. You can also ask us to pack only the tricky rooms and do the rest yourself.
